title: Chapter Thirty: The Guanzhong Plain
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

The decisive battle letter came the morning after.

Short. Blunt. The Tyrant Sword Sect's script, in ink that looked like dried blood.

*Let's have a decisive battle three days after Rain Water.*

End of February. A confrontation that would determine a sect's fate. Entirely different from the sporadic skirmishes of the preceding weeks. A head-on collision between wings of the Thirteen Heavens and the Nine Sects.

Yeon-shin and Won-chang met the Zhongnan Sect leader the day the main masters returned from their latest offensive. In a high-ceilinged hall that smelled of old wood and sword oil, two figures sat at the head of the table.

Yeo Il-sin. The great elder. The woman who had shown him kindness during the Jeong family massacre. Her face hardened when she saw him.

And beside her -- Ou Yang Chen. The Western Heavens Sword Lord. Sect leader of Zhongnan.

He was striking. Sharp, blade-like ears that betrayed his mixed blood -- half-human, half-Elf clan. A yellow long robe. The bearing of a man who had led warriors his entire life. He was said to be seventy, but he did not look past thirty. The son of the Sword Master himself.

"You have done us a favour," Ou Yang Chen said. His voice was solemn, measured. "Jeong Hye is personally watched over by the Sword Immortal. You saved our sect's disciple. We must properly thank you."

His gaze was steady and penetrating.

"Tell me what you want from our sect."

Won-chang trembled beside him. Yeon-shin felt it through the floorboards -- the man's energy spiking with barely contained excitement. He hoped Won-chang would not ask for the golden tea.

"Please allow us to assist in the fight against the Tyrant Sword Sect," Yeon-shin said.

"Hmm."

That was Yeo Il-sin. She looked at him with complex emotions and slightly closed her eyes. He had wanted to ask her about the reason for the Jeong family's annihilation. But he would not spend this opportunity on what was naturally owed.

"Radiant Demon Wing's Lightning Flash," Ou Yang Chen said. "You've comprehended my father's legacy."

A slight interest crossed the sect leader's face.

"He didn't pass on the secret techniques even to his son, saying you were another sect's disciple. It's ironic. To think Desolate Sect would obtain it."

"I only used it as inspiration for my sword technique. I won't learn or spread the Nine Yang Unified Technique."

"How broad-minded." The faintest smile. "I heard you taught the oral chant through Myo-hwa. That too is a great favour. Even if you want the vanguard, we cannot refuse."

The vanguard. The face of the sect in a decisive battle.

Yeon-shin clasped his fists. "I have something separate to ask the Great Elder. I couldn't hear it then due to the circumstances."

Yeo Il-sin's face hardened further. The question about the Jeong family massacre hung in the air between them, but this was not the moment.

---

The wind on the Guanzhong Plain was rough and cold.

A vast expanse of grassland beneath a grey sky. The place where Wei Ji Myo-hwa had earned her title of Sword Dragon by beheading an evil sect's leader.

Yeon-shin stood among the Zhongnan Sect disciples and looked straight ahead.

Two hundred Tyrant Sword warriors. A wall of orange uniforms and drawn blades, the sword-like energy burning off them visible even at a thousand paces. The sect that sought the most tyrannical power in the world.

At their forefront, the absolute being from his memories.

The Tyrant Sword Sect Leader. Expressionless. His great sword thrust into the earth before him. The energy rolling off him was obscene -- a force that pressed against Yeon-shin's senses like a hand on his chest even across the distance.

*Now that I have properly learned martial arts, I understand what an absurd amount of power has been accumulated.*

"So that's Sir Jeong's enemy," Won-chang said. His eyes were hard. He knew the story now. Their friendship had deepened without either of them naming it.

For the first time, Yeon-shin saw in Won-chang an aspect worthy of being called a hero.

He walked forward. Past the rank and file of Zhongnan disciples. To where Ou Yang Chen stood with his sword at his side, staring across the plain.

A figure broke from the Tyrant Sword Sect's line. Young. Orange uniform. A jewelled sword flashing in the pale light.

"I! Third disciple of the Tyrant Sword Sect Leader! Is there a rising talent who can receive Wei Wu-hyeok's sword!"

A vanguard challenge. As common in sect warfare as the decisive duel between veteran masters. Break the enemy's morale by defeating their future.

Yeon-shin stepped to Ou Yang Chen's shoulder.

"I request this with the qualification of having comprehended the legacy of the Sword Master -- the sect leader's father, the great elder's husband. As a guest of the Zhongnan Sect, I will step forward."

"Sir Jeong."

Wei Ji Myo-hwa's voice. He turned. She stood among the Zhongnan elders, and various emotions crossed her face -- admiration, concern, something she could not name and would not speak aloud. She had heard the truth from the Sword Immortal. She knew about the shortened life. She knew what it cost him to burn this brightly.

Yeon-shin gave her a slight bow.

Ou Yang Chen nodded.

"Go ahead."

"Thank you."

He clasped fists. Walked forward. The wind caught his blue robe and white uniform, snapping them behind him like a banner.

Behind him, the Zhongnan Sword Immortal's voice scattered like dust.

"Already a magnificent youth."

---

Wei Wu-hyeok was perhaps seventeen or eighteen. His appearance was transitioning from boy to young man. A crimson martial uniform. A precious sword with a jewelled hilt. And an arrogance so thick it preceded him like a wall.

"The Zhongnan Sect's fate is over," he said.

Yeon-shin said nothing. He remembered this face. The Jeong family manor. The flames. This boy had been there, standing behind his master as the swords came down.

Wei Wu-hyeok looked him up and down. The sneer widened.

"You. I remember. The Jeong family? You showed ridiculous childishness. Should have killed you then, but fortunately you've come before me now."

"You couldn't do anything back then," Yeon-shin said. "Could you."

The sneer faltered. Just a flicker.

"Die in a martial artist's life-and-death battle. What justification would the Tyrant Lord have to intervene? If what you believe in is the same as before, it's truly pitiful."

*The Tyrant Sword Sect Leader's disciple. I can both take revenge and gain achievement.*

Yeon-shin drew the Desolate Sword. His gaze passed over Wei Wu-hyeok's shoulder to the Tyrant Sword Sect Leader standing far behind.

The angular face. The indifferent eyes.

The same as the night the Jeong family burned.

"Now die," Wei Wu-hyeok said, and struck.

No warning. No formal salute. The precious sword came in hard and fast, the full arrogant power of a sect leader's direct disciple behind it.

Yeon-shin met the blade.

The impact detonated. Energy burst outward in a ring of compressed wind that flattened the grass for twenty paces in every direction. Swords locked. Steel screamed against steel.

Wei Wu-hyeok's eyes widened. The force had not yielded.

"Not bad," the boy managed.

Yeon-shin did not answer. He had properly learned sword techniques now, and he could see the flow the sword created. Every habit. Every opening. The way the precious blade tracked slightly left at the end of each arc. The overly committed footwork. The power was real, but the depth was shallow.

He remembered Cheong Myeong testing him when he first entered Radiant Demon Wing.

"Someone from the Elf clan I know said this," Yeon-shin murmured. "They gauge others' talent through sword wind."

"Hm?"

"So this is what it meant."

He smiled.

Radiant Sword Style.

The Desolate Sword caught the sunlight. Truly caught it -- the blade becoming a line of captured radiance that pushed the locked swords apart with a burst of pale fire.

"What--!"

Yeon-shin's hand did not stop. Wind wrapped his entire body as Radiant Wing Step carried him past Wei Wu-hyeok's guard. Through the opening. Along the line of the neck. The white arc of the Desolate Sword flashed once -- diagonally, clean, absolute.

Wei Wu-hyeok's head left his body.

A thud. The body collapsed. The precious sword clattered on the plain.

Yeon-shin sheathed his blade without looking at the corpse.

Silence.

Two hundred Tyrant Sword warriors. A hundred and fifty Zhongnan disciples. The wind. The grass. The vast grey sky above the Guanzhong Plain.

No one moved. No one spoke.

From behind, he felt the weight of the Tyrant Sword Sect Leader's gaze finally settle on him -- not the dismissive glance of a man who had slaughtered his family, but something harder. Something that recognised what was standing on this field.

The pressure at the crown of his skull throbbed once. A dull, familiar ache.

He ignored it.

The battle for Zhongnan had not yet begun.
